entry.title: Debugging SAP Smartforms : Smart tip to debug SAP smartforms
entry.content:
Introduction:
In SAP, Forms vary from SAPscript to Smartforms, Adobe forms, Form templates and Form fragments. Smartforms are quite useful method since 1998 and companies are keeping it frequently till date. But in some situations, any smartform printing starts raising an exception, not giving expected print, giving dump suddenly and we'll keep debugging to find exact NODE for the dump. Here, I would like to share a smart tip to get to the affected NODE within a couple of minutes.

Smart Tip:
I have been debugging ABAP codes from long back whether its standard or custom code. And finally, I had a spot which can show you exactly which NODE in SMARTFORM is having the issue. And I would like to share this knowledge which can save your time and complete the deliveries on time.

Program: SAPLSTXBC or Function Group: STXBC

Include: LSTXBCFDC

Subroutine/Perform: CALLBACK

Here, you just have to pay attention after which NODE your smartform is not giving print or raising an exception when calling from driver program. And that is the NODE which has an issue.

Best Real-time use case:
In migration projects, a few of the complex smartforms have still not been migrated to Adobe Forms while migrating from ECC to S/4 HANA. In such cases, smartforms are either not giving prints or some field mapping has been changed in new system. Or some standard texts do not exist, or some graphics do not exist, not giving expected print, raising an exception or giving dumps. Sometimes, in other scenarios we also have to debug and find the NODE which is causing the issue.

Thanks for reading, Comments are always welcome.
Happy Learning!

entry.title: How to Connect Claude Code to ABAP with Zero-Config MCP
entry.content:
Before We Start - What This Post Deliberately Skips
- What is Claude Code, and what are the available models?
- What is MCP (Model Context Protocol) and how it works conceptually?
- What is vibe coding and why AI-assisted development matters?
- What are the benefits of using AI in SAP development?
- What development platforms are available (Web, Desktop, CLI)?
If you need more details, you can find these topics in sap community portal. Read that, then come back here.

What This Post Covers - and Why It Matters?

This post walks you through connecting Claude Code to a live SAP system using the ABAP Remote Filesystem extension's native MCP server - no external Node.js setup, no manual JSON editing, just a single toggle. 

What this changes for SAP development:

  • No more hand-typed routine code. TYPE declarations, SELECT statements, ALV setup - written directly into the SAP object from a single prompt.
  • No more AI guesswork. Without a live connection, AI tools hallucinate tables and function modules that don't exist. With MCP, every suggestion is grounded in your actual system.
  • No more setup overhead. What used to require a custom Node.js project now takes one checkbox and one command - open to any developer with VS Code.
  • Clean Core by default. Claude works through the same ADT API and released objects that Eclipse uses, so generated code fits your governance model instead of fighting it.

This is the point where AI-assisted ABAP stops being a chat-window experiment and starts being a real part of your IDE.

 
Goal: I will cover the entire topic in 4 parts:
|Part 1| Installing Claude Code on Windows and setting up the VS Code extension |
|Part 2| Connecting VS Code to your live SAP system via the ABAP FS extension |
|Part 3| Enabling the native MCP server with a single toggle - no Node.js setup, no JSON editing |
|Part 4| A live demo: one plain-English prompt creates a real, running ABAP report on SAP |
If you're here, you already know why this matters. Let's get to the actual steps.
 
Pre-requisite: This guide assumes you already have an active Claude subscription (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise). If not, sign up at claude.ai before proceeding
 
Part 1 - The Setup: Getting Claude Code Running on Windows
Step 1.1 - Install Claude Code CLI
Open PowerShell (the built-in Windows command-line shell) and run:

 

This downloads Anthropic's official installer and executes it in one step. 'irm' fetches the script, 'iex' runs it.
> ⚠️ Common Error - Git Bash Not Found
> Claude Code needs a bash shell to run on Windows. If you see this error, install Git for Windows from [git-scm.com/downloads/win](https://git-scm.com/downloads/win). During setup, select: Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software. Then re-run the install command above.
Step 1.2 — Verify the Installation

 

Close PowerShell and open a fresh window. Run:
You should see a version number like 'claude 1.x.x'. If PowerShell says claude is not recognized, the PATH wasn't set automatically. Fix it manually:

 

1. Start → search Environment Variables in system settings
2. Edit the environment variables for your account
3. Select Path → Edit → New → paste ( in this case path can be taken from powershell commnad prompt)
 for eg: 

 

4. Close and reopen PowerShell, then re-run claude --version.

> Run claude doctor - this command checks your PATH, authentication, Node.js version, and config in one shot. Always the first step when something isn't working.
Step 1.3 - Launch Claude Code and the VS Code Extension
Navigate to your project folder and launch Claude Code.
The first launch opens your browser to authenticate with your Anthropic account (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise all work). 
For everyday ABAP work, the VS Code extension is where you'll spend most of your time.
Download and Install VS code from code.visualstudio.com for windows if you don't already have it. Once it is installed.Open VS Code, press 'Ctrl+Shift+X', search for Claude Code (published by Anthropic), and install it. Once installed, the Claude icon appears in the Activity Bar and connects automatically to your account.

 

The extension gives you a persistent chat panel inside VS Code - ask Claude about the file you have open, highlight any ABAP block and ask it to explain or refactor, and switch between ABAP and CAP projects in the same session. No terminal switching required.
> Note on Software Center installs: If your organization restricts installations, Claude Code for VS Code is typically available via your company Software Center / Company Portal.
 
Part 2 - Connecting VS Code to Your SAP System via ABAP FS
This is where the magic begins. The ABAP Remote Filesystem (ABAP FS)extension - built by Marcello Urbani - connects VS Code to your SAP system using the same ADT REST API that Eclipse uses. Once connected, your ABAP objects (programs, classes, CDS views, behavior definitions) appear as files in VS Code. Claude Code can then read, write, and review them like any local code file.
Pre-requisites Before You Connect
You'll need these four things from your SAP system before starting:
| Hostname or IP | The address of your SAP server (hostname preferred - IP causes SSL issues explained below) |
| ADT Service active | Go to transaction SICF → '/sap/bc/adt' must be activated |
| Client number| e.g. '100' - visible in the SAP logon screen |
|Username & Password| Your regular SAP development user |
| Port | HTTPS: '44300' (typical) | HTTP: '8000' for instance 00, '8001' for instance 01 |
 
Step 2.1 - Install and Configure ABAP FS
1. In VS Code Extensions ('Ctrl+Shift+X'), search ABAP remote filesystem by Marcello Urbani → Install.
2. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → type ABAP FS: Connection Manager → open it.
3. Add a new connection and fill in the fields:
   - Name: anything you like (e.g. DEV or SANDBOX)
   - Server URL:'https://<hostname>:44300'
   - ⚠️ Do NOT add '/sap/bc/adt' to the URL - the extension adds this automatically. If you include it, the path doubles and the connection fails.
   - Client, Username, Language: 'EN'
4. Save.
5. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → ABAP FS: Connect to an SAP system→ select your connection → enter password.
 

 

> ⚠️ SSL Certificate Error (very common on internal systems)
> If you see IP does not match certificate's altnames - this is because your SAP system's SSL certificate was issued with a hostname, not the IP. The fix: Get the hostname (e.g. 'https://sapdev.company.com:44300') and use that instead of the IP. 
> ⚠️ADT Service Not Found
> If you see Resource /sap/bc/adt does not exist - the ADT service isn't activated. Run tcode SICF → navigate to default_host → 'sap/bc/adt' → right-click → Activate Service.
 
Step 2.2 - Navigating ABAP Objects
ABAP FS gives you two ways to find objects. Object Search lets you find anything by name instantly. If you prefer a tree view like Eclipse ADT, enable SAP GUI Integration in your connection settings - this gives you a full package tree (Dictionary, Source Code Library, Transactions) and even embeds SAP GUI for HTML directly inside VS Code, so you can run transactions without leaving the editor.
1. Click OBJECT SEARCH in the left sidebar
2. Type the object or package name (e.g. 'ZCL_MY_CLASS' or 'Z_MY_PACKAGE')
3. Double-click any result to open it as a text file in VS Code
4. To edit: right-click  → edit → save (changes push back to SAP automatically)
Once an object is open in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+) and run claude. Some prompts that immediately pay off:

 

Part 3 -The Game Changer: Enabling the Native MCP Server

 

This is the step that changes everything - and the reason this post exists.
Why ABAP FS Alone Isn't Enough?
With only ABAP FS connected, Claude can see only the one file you currently have open. Ask it to create a new class and it might invent table names, function modules, or BAdI definitions that don't exist on your system. It's helpful, but it's guessing.
          ABAP FS Only                                         ABAP FS + MCP
Claude sees what we search Manually Claude autonomously searches any object on the system
Hallucinates objects it hasn't seen Grounded in live SAP system data
You copy-paste code manually Claude reads, writes, activates autonomously
No transport awareness Creates & assigns transports via ADT

 

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server gives Claude Code a live connection to your SAP system's ADT API - the same backend Eclipse uses. Claude stops guessing and starts knowing.
Step 3.1 - Enable the MCP Server (One Toggle)
This is where the zero-config part comes in. You don't need to install anything extra, clone a Node.js repo, or edit JSON files manually.
Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → search ABAP FS MCP → select it → enable Autostart.
That's it. The ABAP FS extension spins up a local MCP server on your machine (default port 4847). You'll see a VS Code notification confirming it's running:
> ABAP MCP Server running on port 4847. External AI clients can connect to http://localhost:4847/mcp

Step 3.2 - Register the MCP Server with Claude Code
Now tell Claude Code where to find the ABAP tools. Run this in the VS Code integrated terminal:
This registers the local MCP server with Claude Code. The configuration is saved to your '.claude.json' file and persists across sessions - you only need to do this once.
Step 3.3 - Verify the Connection

 

open the terminal and run this command once the MCP server is added 
You should see:
Claude Code will confirm when the server is registered:
> abap-remote-fs has been added with HTTP transport at http://localhost:4847/mcp"*
The Complete Architecture
Claude Code can now autonomously:
- Search and read any ABAP object on the live SAP system
- write → syntax check → activate → unlock objects
- Create transport requests and assign objects
- Run ATC checks and read short dumps - all from a plain-English prompt
> Golden - In Auto mode, Claude will write, syntax check, and activate without pausing - that's the power of the agentic workflow. If you want to review before anything is activated, switch to Plan mode  shift+tab to cycle modes)  Claude will show you the full plan and wait for your approval. For production-adjacent systems, always run in Plan or Step mode.
Part 4 - Live Demo: One Prompt, One Real ABAP Program on SAP
Disclaimer: This is my first time setting up all this and explore, so i just started with a very simple scenario. Going forward will create RAP objects and other complex artifacts and see the efficiency.
Let me show you what all of this enables. Here's the exact prompt I used:
> Create a new ABAP executable program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in my ZVS_DEMO package. Fetch data from MARA for a user-specified Material Number (select-option) and display Material Number, Type, and Created By in an ALV grid. Write in ABAP 7.5 syntax.
One prompt. No templates. No copy-paste. Here's what Claude did.
Act 1 - Claude Plans Before It Codes
Claude didn't immediately start writing code. It:
1. Confirmed the target package ('ZVS_DEMO') exists on the system
2. Detected that the program creation triggered a **transport request dialog** — and recognised it was the likely source of a cancellation
3. Called the SAP ADT API via Bash to **list existing open transports**
4. Selected the correct transport and retried the creation
There was a little bit delay on my end to confirm the transport. and it gave me this message.
> Package confirmed. The cancellation is likely a transport request dialog. Let me check for existing transports, then retry.
This is the part that impresses ABAP developers most. Claude handled a real SAP workflow quirk .
Act 2 - The Program Appears on SAP
Claude then created the program on the live system. You can watch its autonomous todo list mid-execution:
- Create ABAP program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in ZVS_DEMO package via MCP
- Get workspace URI for the new program
- Write ABAP source code into the program
- Activate the program and check diagnostics
'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' appeared in the VS Code Explorer sidebar - a real, registered ABAP program on the SAP system, not a local file.
You can see initially there was no program created in my package 
After few seconds, the program is created 

 

Act 3 - Full ABAP 7.5 Source Code Written
With the object created, Claude fetched the workspace URI and wrote the complete source code directly into the SAP program. What it generated:
No placeholder code. Real, working ABAP in the correct syntax release, referencing actual MARA field types from the live system.
Act 4 - Running the Program
After activation, there are two ways to run it:
Option A - Classic SAP GUI:Log in to SAP GUI →run the program → Execute
Option B - Embedded SAP Web GUI(what I used): VS Code can open the SAP Web GUI directly in a browser tab, embedded inside the IDE. No separate login or window switching required.
Enter selection criteria:
The selection screen appeared with the Matnr  field exactly as specified. I entered a range of 11to 21. The value help (F4) shows the available materials in the system
Material Number, Material Type, and Created By - exactly what was asked for. Live data from MARA. Report running.
 
Summary:
|Claude understands SAP context| It identified the transport dialog, queried existing transports via ADT, and handled a real SAP workflow quirk autonomously 
| It created a real SAP object| 'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' exists on the live system, created via ADT REST API through MCP - not a file on disk 
| Production-quality ABAP 7.5 | TYPE declarations referencing actual MARA field types, correct naming conventions, modern SQL syntax 
| Minutes, not hours| The developer's job shifts to reviewing - not writing skeletons, hunting transport dialogs, or looking up field types 
| Fully iterative| Once created, you can ask Claude to add authority checks, error handling, ALV field catalogues, or additional filters - each iteration in seconds 
| You stay in control | Claude's todo list is transparent - you see every step it plans. You review the code. You decide what activates 
 
Where to Go from Here?
Here are five things to try once you have the setup working:
1. Understand inherited code - Explain the purpose and flow of report Z_*_REPORT. Include tables accessed, authority checks, deprecated patterns like SELECT .
2. Generate a CDS view - Create a CDS view hierarchy for Sales Order analysis: interface view joining VBAK+VBAP, projection view with Fiori Elements. UI annotations for a List Report.
3. Build a Travel Request POC -  RAP Business Object with CRUD, approve/reject action, draft handling, OData V4 binding, Fiori Elements UI.
4. Write ABAP Unit tests - Generate a unit test class 
5. Code review and modernization - Review this class for Clean ABAP violations, missing authority checks, SELECT * usage, and SELECT inside LOOP. Propose S/4-equivalent replacements.
 
The bottom line: The integration that used to require manual Node.js setup, JSON editing, and external package management now takes a single Ctrl+Shift+P toggle and one terminal command.
 
The ABAP Remote Filesystem team has done the hard work. Claude Code does the coding. You do the review.
Give it a try - and let me know in the comments what use case you tackle first.
 
A personal note  : This is my first time diving deeper into vibe coding and MCP concepts. I come from a classic ABAP background, and everything covered here -  from the VS Code setup to the MCP server to the live demo - I explored and built from scratch over the past few weeks. The scenario is intentionally simple. The goal was to get the fundamentals right, document them honestly, and share what works. There is a lot more ground to cover - RAP generation at scale, Clean Core extensibility patterns, ATC pre-checks via Claude Code, and deeper agentic workflows. I'll be going further in the posts ahead. If you're also just getting started with this, I hope this gives you a solid, working foundation to build on. And if you're already further ahead - I'd love to hear what you've learnt in the comments.
 
Note: All screenshots in this post are taken from a live S/4HANA development system. Code generated by Claude Code should always be reviewed and tested in a sandbox environment before activation in any productive system.

entry.title: Building a RAP Application with External SAP HANA Cloud using CDS External Entities – Part 1
entry.content:
Why External Entities?

CDS external entities provide a modern way to handle secondary database connections in ABAP Cloud. They allow applications to retrieve and modify data from other databases using SQL. Sounds interesting? This blog post covers how to create a RAP application in SAP BTP ABAP Environment  with a secondary HANA database connection.

 

Business scenario:


Many organizations already have business data stored in a separate SAP HANA Cloud database and want to build modern RAP (ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model) applications on SAP BTP ABAP Environment without replicating that data into the ABAP system.

The blog demonstrates this using a Loyalty Management application.

Scenario

  • Customer loyalty information (Memberships) is stored in an external SAP HANA Cloud database.
  • Transaction data related to each membership is also stored in the same external database.
  • The business wants a Fiori application where users can:
    • Create loyalty memberships
    • Maintain loyalty transactions
    • Update and delete records
    • View accumulated loyalty points
    • Persist all changes directly in the external HANA database

 

Prerequisites:

  1. SAP BTP Sub-Account
  2. SAP HANA Cloud Instance
  3. SAP BTP ABAP Environment

Steps:

  1. Setup SAP HANA Cloud instance
  2. Create Schema and Tables
  3. Configure external connection to External HANA database from SAP BTP ABAP Environment using External Entities
  4. Create External Entities

Setup SAP HANA Cloud instance

Let’s start on with the creation of SAP HANA Cloud instance.

  1. Open SAP BTP cockpit and add the following Entitlements.



  2. Create HANA cloud subscription.


  3. Create HANA Cloud Instance


    Specify Instance name, Set DBADMIN password and choose Allow all IP address
    Spoiler
    Disclaimer on Database IP Access Configuration
    Allowing unrestricted access (e.g., permitting all IP addresses such as 0.0.0.0/0) to a database that contains production data introduces significant security risks. This approach should only be used in strictly controlled environments.


  4. Once the Instance status turns into Running state and click on Actions three dots and Open in SAP HANA Database Explorer. 
    Provide the DBADMIN credentials from the previous step to connect to the HANA database.


 

Create Schema and Tables

Open SQL Console and perform the following steps to create the schema and tables.
  • Create Schema

CREATE SCHEMA LOYALTY;​

  • Create Membership table: zlm_membship_ee  .

Stores membership details
Primary key: client + membershipuuid

create column table loyalty."zlm_membship_ee" (
"client" nvarchar (000003) default '000' not null, 
"membershipuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null , 
"membershipid" nvarchar (000010) default '0000000000' not null, 
"customer" nvarchar (000010) default '' not null, 
"createdby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"createdat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"locallastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null,
primary key ("client", "membershipuuid" )     
) column loadable
​
  • Create Transactions table: zlm_transctns_ee

Stores transactions linked to membership.
Contains transaction value and loyalty points.

create column table loyalty."zlm_transctns_ee" (
"client" nvarchar (000003) default '000' not null, 
"transactionuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null ,
"membershipuuid" varbinary (000016) default x'00000000000000000000000000000000' not null , 
"transactiondate" NVARCHAR (000008) DEFAULT '00000000' NOT NULL, 
"transactionvalue" DECIMAL (000010, 000002) DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL, 
"transactioncurrency" NVARCHAR (000005) DEFAULT '' NOT NULL, 
"loyaltypoints" BIGINT DEFAULT 0 NOT NULL, 
"createdby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"createdat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedby" nvarchar (000012) default '' not null, 
"locallastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null, 
"lastchangedat" decimal (000021, 000007) default 0 not null,
primary key ("client", "transactionuuid" )     
) column loadable

​


Configure connectivity to an external SAP HANA database from Steampunk for data access using External Entities

  1. Establish outbound SQL access using an external entity using ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse

    1. Define and activate a logical external schema whose configuration specifies the connection details for the outbound communication. Create a logical external schema in ABAP Development Tools (ADT) using the creation wizard.


    2.  Create an Outbound Service of the type Outbound SQL Access for your logical external schema.


    3. Create a Communication Scenario.


    4. Include the outbound service in the communication scenario.


  2. Configure External Connection
    In the Fiori Launchpad, Logon as Administrator and perform the following steps.
    1. Create a Communication Arrangement for scenario ZLM_COM_EXT_SCHEMA_EE.




    2.  Create a Communication System



      Toggle Remote SQL Access and choose Adapter Name as HANA (ODBC) 
      Adapter Configuration :Driver=libodbcHDB.so;ServerNode=*.hanacloud.ondemand.com(URL of the External Database):443;



    3. Create an Outbound User. Specify the HANA DB user which contains the necessary authorizations for accessing the External Schema.




Create CDS External Entities

Two types:
1. Writable External Entity → Used for INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE
2. Static External Entity → Used for SELECT (read only)

These entities map external tables to ABAP CDS.

In ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse: As an ABAP Developer, here’s what you need to do to establish outbound SQL access using a CDS external entity.

  • Define and activate an external entity that represents the external database object.As we are going to perform CUD operation on external database, we need to create Writable External Entity for table zlm_membship_ee which will be referred by external name zlm_membship_ee.
@EndUserText.label: 'Writable EE for HANA tab zlm_membship_ee'
define writable external entity zlm_membship_cud_ee external name "zlm_membship_ee"
{
  key client             : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key membershipuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      membershipid       : zlm_membershipid_ee           external name "membershipid";
      customer           : zlm_customer_ee               external name "customer";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby          : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat          : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby      : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat      : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided at runtime
​
​
  • We need to create Writable External Entity for table zlm_transctns_ee
@EndUserText.label: 'Writable EE for HANAtab zlm_transctns_ee'
define writable external entity zlm_transctns_cud_ee external name "zlm_transctns_ee"
{
  key client              : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key transactionuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "transactionuuid";
      membershipuuid      : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      transactiondate     : zlm_transactiondate_ee        external name "transactiondate";
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'transactioncurrency'
      transactionvalue    : zlm_transactionvalue_ee       external name "transactionvalue";
      transactioncurrency : zlm_transactioncurrency_ee    external name "transactioncurrency";
      loyaltypoints       : zlm_loyaltypoints_ee          external name "loyaltypoints";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby           : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat           : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby       : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat  : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided at runtime
​
​
  • To securely map a remote physical database object so it can be queried directly, we need to use STATIC EXTERNAL ENTITIES to fetch data from table zlm_membship_ee .
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Static EE for HANA tab zlm_membship_ee'
define external entity zlm_membship_ee external name "zlm_membship_ee"
{
  key client             : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key membershipuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      membershipid       : zlm_membershipid_ee           external name "membershipid";
      customer           : zlm_customer_ee               external name "customer";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby          : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat          : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby      : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat      : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided by zlm_ext_schema_ee
​
​
  • We need another STATIC EXTERNAL ENTITY for table zlm_transctns_ee
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Static EE for HANA tab zlm_transctns_ee'
define external entity zlm_transctns_ee external name "zlm_transctns_ee"
{
  key client              : abap.char(3)                  external name "client";
  key transactionuuid     : sysuuid_x16                   external name "transactionuuid";
      membershipuuid      : sysuuid_x16                   external name "membershipuuid";
      transactiondate     : zlm_transactiondate_ee        external name "transactiondate";
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode : 'transactioncurrency'
      transactionvalue    : zlm_transactionvalue_ee       external name "transactionvalue";
      transactioncurrency : zlm_transactioncurrency_ee    external name "transactioncurrency";
      loyaltypoints       : zlm_loyaltypoints_ee          external name "loyaltypoints";
      @Semantics.user.createdBy: true
      createdby           : abp_creation_user             external name "createdby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.createdAt: true
      createdat           : abp_creation_tstmpl           external name "createdat";
      @Semantics.user.lastChangedBy: true
      lastchangedby       : abp_lastchange_user           external name "lastchangedby";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.localInstanceLastChangedAt: true
      locallastchangedat  : abp_locinst_lastchange_tstmpl external name "locallastchangedat";
      @Semantics.systemDateTime.lastChangedAt: true
      lastchangedat       : abp_lastchange_tstmpl         external name "lastchangedat";
}
with federated data provided by zlm_ext_schema_ee

​

Key Takeaways.

External entities enable real-time integration with external databases without replication.

In Part 2 of this series, we will build a RAP application using the data model created here.

entry.title: Why Stock Fields Return Zero in Custom CDS Views on MARC - and How to Resolve It
entry.content:
Why Stock Fields Return Zero in Custom CDS Views on MARC - and How to Resolve It

I was building a custom CDS view on the MARC table (Plant Data for Material) to extract data into SAP Datasphere using Change Data Capture (CDC) for delta loads.

After activating the view, I checked the data and found something odd. The field TRAME (Stock in Transit) had a proper value in the MARC table, but in my CDS view the same field was completely empty.

To give a concrete example, for one material and plant the MARC table showed TRAME = 50.000, but my CDS view showed TRAME = 0.000 for the same key. All the master data fields were perfectly fine. Only the stock field was wrong.

That single clue — master data correct, stock field zero — is what pointed me to the real cause.


Where the data actually lives in S/4HANA

In the older SAP ECC system, stock quantities were physically saved in many tables, and the same figure was often repeated in several places. This created redundancy and slowed things down.

In S/4HANA, SAP redesigned this part of the system with the New Simplified Data Model (NSDM). The idea is straightforward: all stock movements are written into a single table called MATDOC, and the stock figures are calculated when you ask for them instead of being stored everywhere.

Because of this change, MARC became a so-called hybrid table. It still physically stores the material master and planning data, but the stock quantity fields are no longer filled in the table itself. Fields like TRAME, UMLMC, GLGMG and BWESB are now worked out on the fly from MATDOC whenever they are read.

So in S/4HANA, the TRAME column inside the physical MARC table is basically empty. The real stock-in-transit figure is calculated from MATDOC only at the moment it is needed.

Why SE16 showed the value but my CDS did not

This is the most important point of the whole story. SE16 and my CDS view were reading the same table through two completely different paths.

When you open MARC in SE16, the read goes through the normal application layer. SAP automatically redirects that read to a proxy object behind MARC, which performs the MATDOC calculation and returns the correct TRAME value. That is why SE16 happily showed 50.000.

My CDS view, on the other hand, was written as select from marc. A CDS view runs straight on the database and reads the physical MARC table directly. It never passes through the redirect, so it picked up the empty physical column and returned 0.000.

In one sentence:

SE16 reads MARC through the proxy and gets the calculated value. A CDS that selects directly from MARC reads the raw table and gets nothing. That difference in the read path was the entire reason the data "did not come."


My first fix — and why it failed

My first idea was simple. If the proxy returns the correct value, why not read from the proxy instead of the table? So I changed the source of my view to the proxy object:

Before (TRAME comes as zero):

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_MARC_DEX'

@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true

@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

@EndUserText.label: 'Plant Data for Material'

@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true

@analytics.dataExtraction:{enabled: true,

delta.changeDataCapture:{mapping:[

{table:'marc',role:#MAIN,

viewElement:['Matnr','Werks'],

tableElement:['matnr','werks']}]}}

define view ZCDS_MARC_DEX_CDC as select from marc

{

   key matnr as Matnr,

   key werks as Werks,

   pstat as Pstat,

   lvorm as Lvorm,

   bwtty as Bwtty,

   xchar as Xchar,

   trame as Trame,

... remaining MARC fields ...

After (TRAME correct and CDC activates):

@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZCDS_MARC_DEX'

@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true

@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED

@EndUserText.label: 'Plant Data for Material'

@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true 

@analytics:{

dataExtraction.enabled: true,

dataExtraction.delta.changeDataCapture:{

mapping:[

   {

      table:'marc',

      role:#MAIN,

      viewElement:['Matnr','Werks'],

      tableElement:['matnr','werks']

   }]

}

}

define view ZCDS_MARC_DEX_CDC as select from nsdm_e_marc

{

   key matnr as Matnr,

   key werks as Werks,

   pstat as Pstat,

   lvorm as Lvorm,

   bwtty as Bwtty, 

   xchar as Xchar,

   trame as Trame,

... remaining MARC fields ...

The only real change is the data source: from marc to nsdm_e_marc. The CDC mapping still refers to the physical table marc


This is not only a MARC problem

The same behaviour happens with all NSDM stock tables in S/4HANA. If your CDS reads stock fields straight from any of these, you can get zeros:

  • MARC – plant stock fields (TRAME, UMLMC, GLGMG, BWESB)
  • MARD – storage location stock
  • MCHB – batch stock
  • MKOL – special / consignment stock
  • MSKA – sales order stock
  • MSKU – customer special stock
  • MSLB – stock with vendor (subcontracting)
  • MSPR, MSSA, MSSQ – project and sales order stock totals
  • MSTB, MSTE, MSTQ – stock in transit tables


A simple rule of thumb: if a table held stock quantities in ECC, in S/4HANA those quantities are now calculated, and a direct CDS read will return zero. Use the table's proxy instead.

How to find the correct proxy in your own system

You do not have to guess the name. For any table (example: MARC):

  1. Open the table in SE11.
  2. Choose menu Extras → Replacement Object to see the assigned proxy (for MARC it is NSDM_E_MARC).

Example – "MARC" Replacement Object

Conclusion

What first looked like a bug in my CDS view turned out to be normal S/4HANA behaviour. Under the New Simplified Data Model, stock quantity fields are no longer stored in tables like MARC — they are calculated from MATDOC only when they are read. SE16 shows the correct value because it goes through the replacement object, while a CDS view that reads the table directly picks up the empty physical column and returns zero.

The fix itself is small but important: source the CDS view from the replacement object (for MARC, NSDM_E_MARC) instead of the base table. After that one change, TRAME returned the correct value. And because this is standard NSDM behaviour, the same approach works for every stock table in the family — whenever a stock field comes back as zero, the first thing to check is whether you are reading the base table directly instead of its replacement object.

The bigger lesson I took away is to always confirm where a field's data actually lives before building a CDS view on top of a table. A quick look at SE11 → Extras → Replacement Object tells you whether a table is a hybrid table and which object you should really be reading from. That single check can save a lot of confusing "the value is there in SE16 but missing in my view" situations.

entry.title: Building a Production-Ready Custom ATC Check: Enforcing ABAP Unit Coverage
entry.content:
Introduction

Many development teams encourage ABAP Unit adoption, but enforcing minimum test coverage is still a challenge.

During a recent project, I needed a way to prevent the release of new developments that did not meet a minimum coverage threshold.

Since I couldn't find a standard ATC check that fulfilled this requirement, I decided to build a custom ATC validation.

The final solution became a lightweight and configurable quality gate that can be integrated directly into existing SCI and ATC processes.

Inspiration

Before starting the implementation, I analyzed several open-source ATC extensions, especially the SAP Code Pal for ABAP project.

Code Pal is an excellent reference for understanding how custom checks can be integrated into the ATC framework and how configurable validations can be implemented.

However, my goal was not to build a generic quality framework. Instead, I wanted a lightweight solution focused on a single requirement: enforcing a minimum ABAP Unit test coverage threshold.

For this reason, I implemented a simplified check that leverages the standard SCI/ATC framework and ABAP Unit coverage APIs while remaining easy to maintain and extend.

Understanding the Solution

The solution is composed of three main components:

  • A custom ATC category
  • A custom ATC check
  • A configurable SCI/ATC variant

The execution flow is illustrated below:

SCI / ATC
      ↓
Custom Check
      ↓
ABAP Unit Coverage Analysis
      ↓
Threshold Validation
      ↓
ATC Finding

Creating a Custom Check Category

The first step is to create a custom category that will group all checks.

Create a class that inherits from CL_CI_CATEGORY_ROOT.

METHOD constructor.
  super->constructor( ).

  description = TEXT-t01.
  category    = me->ci_category.
ENDMETHOD.

This category will later appear in the SCI administration screen and will act as a container for all custom validations.

Creating the Check Class

Next, create the actual validation check by inheriting from CL_CI_TEST_ROOT.

In the constructor, define the basic metadata of the check:

  • Description
  • Category
  • Display position
  • Version
  • Error messages
METHOD constructor.
  super->constructor( ).

  description = `Minimum ABAP Unit Coverage`.
  category    = `Your Custom Category`.
  position    = `010`.
  version    += 1.

  APPEND VALUE #(
           sign   = `I`
           option = `EQ`
           low    = `1PRG`
         ) TO me->typelist.

  APPEND VALUE #(
           test = me->myname
           code = `CI001`
           kind = me->c_error
           text = 'Insufficient coverage: &1% (min. &2%)'
         ) TO me->scimessages.
ENDMETHOD.

Once activated and registered in SCI, the category and the check should appear in the SCI management screen.

New checks

Category and new check

Adding Configuration Parameters

One of the main goals of this implementation was to make the validation configurable.

Instead of hardcoding values, the minimum coverage threshold and object filters are maintained through the SCI variant.

To achieve this, I redefined the method IF_CI_TEST~QUERY_ATTRIBUTES.

This allows us to build a custom parameter screen such as:

  • Minimum Coverage (%)
  • Created After
  • Object Type
  • BAdI Implementations Only
  • RAP Behavior Implementations Only
METHOD if_ci_test~query_attributes.
  DATA(attributes) = VALUE sci_atttab(
    ...
  ).

  DATA(break) =
    cl_ci_query_attributes=>generic(
      p_name       = me->myname
      p_title      = me->description
      p_attributes = attributes
      p_display    = p_display ).
ENDMETHOD.

The resulting configuration screen looks like this:

Screen

Persisting Variant Parameters

The values entered by users need to be stored and restored whenever the variant is executed.

This is done by redefining the methods GET_ATTRIBUTES and PUT_ATTRIBUTES.

METHOD get_attributes.
  EXPORT attributes_filled = attributes_filled
    TO DATA BUFFER p_attributes.
ENDMETHOD.

METHOD put_attributes.
  IMPORT attributes_filled = attributes_filled
    FROM DATA BUFFER p_attributes.
ENDMETHOD.

Implementing the Validation Logic

The heart of every ATC check is the RUN method.

This method is executed for every analyzed object.

METHOD run.
  CHECK me->program_name IS NOT INITIAL.
  CHECK me->get( ) = me->c_true.

  CHECK me->object_type IN me->attributes_filled-object_type.
  CHECK me->is_after_minimum_creation_date( ) >=
        me->attributes_filled-date_from.

  DATA(percentage) = me->get_coverage( ).

  IF percentage < me->attributes_filled-coverage_minimun.

    inform(
      p_sub_obj_type = me->c_type_include
      p_sub_obj_name = me->program_name
      p_kind         = me->c_error
      p_test         = me->myname
      p_code         = `CI001`
      p_param_1      = |{ percentage NUMBER = USER }|
      p_param_2      = |{ me->attributes_filled-coverage_minimun NUMBER = USER }|
    ).
  ENDIF.
ENDMETHOD.

Measuring ABAP Unit Coverage

One of the most interesting parts of this implementation was discovering that ABAP Unit coverage can be measured programmatically.

The class CL_AUCV_TASK provides the necessary APIs.

METHOD get_coverage.
  DATA(aunit) =
    cl_aucv_task=>create(
      i_measure_coverage = abap_true ).

  ...

  percentage =
    branch_coverage->get_percentage( ).
ENDMETHOD.

This allows the ATC check to compare the actual coverage with the threshold configured in the SCI variant.

Executing the Check

After creating an SCI variant and selecting the custom check, simply configure the desired threshold.

For example:

  • Created After = 01.01.2025
  • Minimum Coverage = 85%
  • Object Type = CLAS

First, let's measure the ABAP Unit coverage of a sample object:

ABAP Unit

When ATC is executed, findings are generated whenever the object's coverage is below the required percentage.

ATC Result

Lessons Learned

  • ATC is far more extensible than it initially appears.
  • Custom checks can be used to implement real governance requirements.
  • ABAP Unit coverage can be measured programmatically.
  • Configurable checks simplify gradual adoption.
  • Quality gates are an effective way to increase test adoption.

Final Thoughts

By combining the SCI framework, ATC extensibility and ABAP Unit coverage APIs, it is possible to build lightweight but powerful quality controls directly inside the SAP development process.

In addition to ATC execution, I also integrated this validation into the transport release process, ensuring that new developments cannot be released when they fail to meet the required coverage threshold.

Have you ever implemented a custom ATC validation in your landscape? Feel free to share your experience in the comments.

entry.title: Extending the Fiori App Display Supplier List (F1861): A Practical Approach Using CDS Views
entry.content:
Introduction

Extending standard SAP Fiori applications is a common requirement in S/4HANA projects.

In a recent scenario, I needed to include additional supplier fields from table LFA1 into the standard app Display Supplier List (F1861). However, even though the fields existed in the backend, they were not available in the Fiori application.

The Challenge

At first glance, extending a standard app may seem straightforward, but when dealing with CDS-based applications, simply adding fields at database level is not enough.

The F1861 app relies on CDS views and requires specific extension steps to properly expose additional fields.

SAP Note

According to SAP Note 3200905, extending standard CDS-based applications requires following a specific approach to ensure that custom fields are correctly exposed in both UI and analytical layers.

3200905 - Display Supplier List: Standard extensibility delivered by View Entity

Approach Overview

To address this, the following approach was implemented:

  • Extend the CDS view used as data source (C_Supplier)
  • Extend the consumption CDS view (C_SupplierList)
  • Apply UI annotations via Metadata Extension

Implementation

The standard interface view C_Supplier was extended to include custom fields from table LFA1.

extend view C_Supplier with ZEXT_C_SUPPLIER
  association [1..1] to lfa1 as _Supplier on $projection.supplier = _Supplier.lifnr
{
  _Supplier.ort02                                         as ExtDistrictName,
  _Supplier.brsch                                         as ExtIndustry,
  cast( _Supplier.gbdat as zext_gbdat preserving type )   as ExtBirthDate, // Custom data element used – standard conversion exit PDATE is not supported in CDS
  _Supplier.txjcd                                         as ExtTaxJurisdiction,
  _Supplier.regss                                         as SupplIsSocialInsuranceRegtrd,
  _Supplier.actss                                         as SocialInsuranceActivityCode,
  _Supplier.cnae                                          as ExtTaxEconomicActivityCode
}

A custom data element was created for the birth date field, as the standard conversion exit (PDATE) is not supported in CDS views. This allowed proper handling of the field without relying on conversion exits.

Next, the consumption view C_SupplierList was extended:

extend view C_SupplierList with ZEXT_C_SUPPLIERLIST
{
  cast( '' as ort02_gp )                         as ExtDistrictName,
  cast( '' as brsch )                            as ExtIndustry,
  cast( '' as zext_gbdat )                       as ExtBirthDate,
  cast( '' as txjcd )                            as ExtTaxJurisdiction,
  cast( '' as j_1aregss preserving type )        as SupplIsSocialInsuranceRegtrd,
  cast( '' as j_1aactss )                        as SocialInsuranceActivityCode,
  cast( '' as j_1bcnae )                         as ExtTaxEconomicActivityCode
}

A metadata extension was then used to control the visibility of fields in the UI:

@Metadata.layer: #CORE
annotate view C_SupplierList with
{
  @UI: { hidden: true }
  BPTaxType;

  @UI: { hidden: true }
  VATRegistration;

  @UI: { hidden: true }
  TaxTypeName;
}

This approach ensures that the extension remains upgrade-safe and aligned with SAP extensibility guidelines.

Result

After the extensions and annotations, the custom fields were successfully displayed in the F1861 app.

Display Supplier List (new fields)

Challenges Faced

Some challenges were encountered during the implementation:

  • Fields not appearing due to missing annotations
  • Issues with CDS associations
  • Inconsistent behavior when extending standard views
  • Limitations when reusing associations locally

Key Learnings

This scenario highlights several important aspects when working with SAP extensibility:

  • Understanding the CDS view hierarchy is essential
  • Metadata Extensions are required for UI exposure
  • SAP Notes often contain critical implementation details

Final Thoughts

This scenario demonstrates how standard SAP applications can be extended in a structured way, without modifying the core system, following clean core principles.

I would be interested to hear how other teams are handling Fiori extensibility scenarios.

👉Feel free to share your approach or exchange ideas.

entry.title: How to Find the Right Enhancement Spot in Fiori App F3516: A Practical Validation Scenario
entry.content:
Introduction

Validations are a common requirement in SAP Fiori applications. However, identifying the correct enhancement spot for a given app can be challenging.

The Challenge

In this scenario, a validation was required in the Fiori app Manage Profit Centers (Version 2) - F3516 to ensure that the segment value followed specific business rules.

The main challenge was not implementing the validation itself, but identifying the correct enhancement point in the backend.

How the Enhancement Spot Was Identified

A practical approach I often use is to identify the technical package behind the Fiori application.

This can be done by navigating to the Fiori Apps Library, checking the Implementation Information section, and identifying the OData service used by the application.

From there, the OData service can be searched in the ABAP environment (Eclipse), allowing identification of the corresponding package.

In this case, within the application package ODATA_PROFIT_CENTER_V2, an enhancement package with a very intuitive name was found:

ODATA_PROFIT_CENTER_V2_ENH (Enhancements for Manage Profit Center)

CLASS CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX

Within this package, it was possible to identify available enhancement implementations. Among the options, the following example class was used as a reference:

class CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX definition
  public
  final
  create public.

public section.

  interfaces IF_BADI_INTERFACE.
  interfaces IF_BADI_FIN_PC_VALIDATION.

protected section.
private section.
ENDCLASS.
CLASS CL_FIN_PC_VALIDATION_IMPL_EX IMPLEMENTATION.

  method IF_BADI_FIN_PC_VALIDATION~EXECUTE.

    data: ls_validationmessage like line of ct_messages.

    ls_validationmessage-msgid = 'FAC_MPC_PCTR'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgno = '022'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgty = 'E'.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv1 = 'The Field '.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv2 = 'Profit Center Category '.
    ls_validationmessage-msgv3 = 'contains invalid value '.

    insert ls_validationmessage into table ct_messages.

  endmethod.

ENDCLASS.

Implementation

The validation logic was implemented to ensure that segment values starting with "0" are not allowed:

METHOD if_badi_fin_pc_validation~execute.
    CONSTANTS: BEGIN OF segment_values,
                 geral   TYPE fb_segment VALUE `GERAL`,
                 ato     TYPE fb_segment VALUE `ATO`,
                 nao_ato TYPE fb_segment VALUE `NAO_ATO`,
               END OF segment_values.

    CHECK is_profit_center-profitcenterforedit+09(01) CA `012`.

    DATA(segment) = SWITCH fb_segment( is_profit_center-profitcenterforedit+09(01) WHEN `0` THEN segment_values-geral
                                                                                   WHEN `1` THEN segment_values-ato
                                                                                   WHEN `2` THEN segment_values-nao_ato ).

    IF is_profit_center-segment <> segment.
      INSERT VALUE #( msgid = `ZFIN`
                      msgty = `E`
                      msgno = 001
                      msgv1 = segment ) INTO TABLE ct_messages.
    ENDIF.
  ENDMETHOD.

This validation logic was implemented within the BAdI execution method, ensuring that the business rule is enforced during the save process.

Result

When the validation is triggered, the system prevents saving and displays an error message to the user.

Fiori error message

Key Insight

The most critical part of this implementation was identifying the correct enhancement point.

Once the correct spot was found, the validation itself was straightforward.

This reinforces that, in many SAP extensibility scenarios, the main challenge is not writing the logic itself, but navigating the framework to identify where that logic should be implemented.

Final Thoughts

This scenario highlights that, in many cases, the real challenge is not the implementation itself, but understanding where and how to implement it within the SAP framework.

👉I would be interested to hear how others approach similar challenges.

entry.title: Making Interface Methods Optional in ABAP: DEFAULT IGNORE vs DEFAULT FAIL
entry.content:
Introduction

If you've worked with OO ABAP for a while, you've probably hit this situation: you define an interface with a handful of methods, and suddenly every single class that implements it is forced to write a method body even for stuff that class doesn't care about. Multiply that across a dozen implementing classes and you end up with a pile of empty, do-nothing method stubs just to keep the compiler happy.

ABAP actually has a clean way around this, and it's one of those features that doesn't get talked about enough, the DEFAULT IGNORE and DEFAULT FAIL additions on interface method declarations. Once you know about them, you'll wonder how you lived without them.

Picture an interface for handling pricing logic. Something like:

INTERFACE zif_pricing. 
 METHODS check_pricing_needed. 
 METHODS run_calculation 
   DEFAULT IGNORE. 
 METHODS fetch_base_amount 
   RETURNING VALUE(rv_amount) TYPE p 
   DEFAULT FAIL. 
ENDINTERFACE.

Not every class that plugs into zif_pricing will need all three methods. Maybe check_pricing_needed is genuinely required everywhere, but run_calculation only matters for a subset of classes, and fetch_base_amount is something you want classes to be forced to think about if they ever try to use it.

Rather than making every implementer write empty stubs, you can tell ABAP what should happen at runtime if a method is left unimplemented. That's what these two additions do, they shift the "what if this isn't implemented" decision from compile-time enforcement to a runtime behavior you control.

DEFAULT IGNORE: silently do nothing

Tag a method with DEFAULT IGNORE and here's the deal: if a class skips implementing it, calling that method just... does nothing. No error, no dump, nothing. It behaves exactly as if someone had written an empty METHOD ... ENDMETHOD. block. Any returning parameter just comes back with its initial value, and the program keeps going like nothing happened.

This is great for the genuinely optional stuff things where "not implemented" and "nothing to do here" mean the same thing. Logging hooks, audit trail updates, optional enhancement points, extra notifications anywhere a no op is a perfectly acceptable outcome.

DEFAULT FAIL: blow up loudly instead

DEFAULT FAIL is the opposite philosophy. If the implementing class doesn't provide a body for that method, calling it raises a runtime exception, specifically CX_SY_DYN_CALL_ILLEGAL_METHOD. Leave that exception unhandled and you'll get a short dump (CALL_METHOD_NOT_IMPLEMENTED).

This sounds harsh until you think about why you'd want it. There's a real difference between "this method isn't relevant for this class" and "this class genuinely needs this logic but someone forgot to write it." DEFAULT FAIL exists for the second case it converts a silent gap in your business logic into something that screams at you the moment it's hit, instead of quietly limping along and producing wrong results downstream.

Tax calculations, price determination, approval logic, validation anything where skipping the logic isn't a valid outcome, just a bug waiting to be found is a good candidate for DEFAULT FAIL.

Walking through it

Define the interface with the optional methods marked accordingly:

INTERFACE zif_pricing.
  METHODS check_pricing_needed.
  METHODS run_calculation
    DEFAULT IGNORE.
  METHODS fetch_base_amount
    RETURNING VALUE(rv_amount) TYPE p
    DEFAULT FAIL.
ENDINTERFACE.

Implement it in a class, but only bother with the mandatory method:

CLASS zcl_pricing DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES zif_pricing.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_pricing IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD zif_pricing~check_pricing_needed.
    " actual business logic goes here
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Notice there's no METHOD zif_pricing~run_calculation or METHOD zif_pricing~fetch_base_amount anywhere in this class and that's completely fine. The compiler only insists on an implementation for methods that don't carry a DEFAULT addition.

Call the IGNORE method and watch nothing bad happen:

DATA(lo_pricing) = NEW zcl_pricing( ).
lo_pricing->zif_pricing~run_calculation( ).

Since this one was never implemented but is marked DEFAULT IGNORE, the call just passes through quietly same as calling an empty method.

Call the FAIL method and you'll get an exception:

DATA(lv_amount) = lo_pricing->zif_pricing~fetch_base_amount( ).

Because fetch_base_amount is DEFAULT FAIL and was never implemented, this line throws CX_SY_DYN_CALL_ILLEGAL_METHOD. Leave it unhandled and the program dumps.

Handle it properly instead of letting it dump:

TRY.
    DATA(lv_amount) = lo_pricing->zif_pricing~fetch_base_amount( ).
  CATCH cx_sy_dyn_call_illegal_method INTO DATA(lx_err).
    MESSAGE lx_err->get_text( ) TYPE 'I'.
ENDTRY.

Wrapping the call in a TRY/CATCH lets you turn that hard failure into a controlled message instead of a dump in production.

Why bother with DEFAULT FAIL at all — isn't IGNORE always safer?

It feels safer on the surface, but that's actually the trap. Say you've got:

METHODS send_notification DEFAULT IGNORE.

If a class forgets to implement this, calling send_notification( ) just silently does nothing. No email goes out, no alert fires and the calling code has no idea anything went wrong. That's the kind of bug that sits undetected for months because everything looks fine from the outside.

Mark it DEFAULT FAIL instead, and a forgotten implementation gets caught the first time it's actually exercised, not three releases later when someone's wondering why customers never got their notification emails.

Rule of thumb for picking one

Go with IGNORE when "not implemented" and "nothing happens" are an acceptable, sensible default logging, auditing, optional enrichment, extension hooks.

Go with FAIL when skipping the logic would produce incorrect or incomplete business behavior pricing, tax, approvals, workflow triggers, validations. Anywhere being wrong silently is worse than crashing loudly.

A few things to keep in mind

  • These additions only apply inside interface declarations you can't slap DEFAULT IGNORE or DEFAULT FAIL on a regular class method.
  • Constructors and test methods can't be made optional this way.
  • Both work fine whether the method is instance bound or static.
  • They're also usable in BAdI interface definitions, which is honestly where I think they shine the most BAdIs love having a long list of methods where only a couple are relevant to any given implementation.

Bottom line

DEFAULT IGNORE and DEFAULT FAIL give you a way to design genuinely flexible interfaces without sacrificing safety. Instead of forcing every implementer to write boilerplate empty methods, you decide upfront, right in the interface definition, what "not implemented" should mean for each method a harmless no op, or a loud, catchable failure. Used well, it keeps your interfaces lean and your bugs visible instead of buried.

entry.title: SAP Developer News, July 9th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS

Live from WeAreDevelopers

- WeAreDevelopers World Congress https://www.wearedevelopers.com/world-congress

CAP June 2026 release (cds 10)

- Release notes https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/releases/2026/jun26

ABAP Cleaner in VS Code!

- https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=SAPOSS.abap-cleaner

Live streams at Code Connect next week

- Code Connect https://code-connect.dev/
- UI5con https://openui5.org/ui5con/
- UI5con agenda https://openui5.org/ui5con/program.html
- UI5con 2026 Live from the Main Stage https://www.youtube.com/live/CMPudw4scSE
- reCAP https://recap-conf.dev/
- reCAP agenda https://recap-conf.dev/program.html
- reCAP Live from the Main Stage https://broadcast.sap.com/go/reCAP
- reCAP 2026 - Streaming Sidetrack https://teams.microsoft.com/meet/347149883889697?p=ZkR4erzGjpPRtZ6zJh

Additional CAP highlights and MCP Gateway

- Building MCP Servers with SAP Integration Suite: What to Design, Not Just How to Connect https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/building-mcp-servers-with-sap-integration-suite-what-to-design-not-just-how/ba-p/14435261
- Design APIs and MCP servers https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/design-apis?locale=en-US
- Release calendar for SAP Integration Suite https://me.sap.com/notes/3418154

Did You Know? #25 cds add test

- The cds add command https://cap.cloud.sap/docs/tools/cds-cli#cds-add

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro
00:07 Live from WeAreDevelopers
02:16 CAP June 2026 release (cds 10)
13:14 ABAP Cleaner in VS Code!
14:37 Live streams at Code Connect next week
15:05 Additional CAP highlights and MCP Gateway
17:20 DYK #25 cds add test

TRANSCRIPT

[Intro]
This is the Developer News for July 9th 2026.

[Tom, Sheena, Shilpa, Witalij]
Hi, we're coming to you this week from WeAreDevelopers conference in Berlin, which is a little bit of a difference for us developer advocates because we're actually mostly attending a conference instead of delivering one. So it's our opportunity to connect with other developers, particularly non-SAP developers from all around the world. I'm giving a session on Friday with Michael Ameling, where I'll be doing a preview of the new developers.sap.com that's all AI-powered - we'll show you more of in the coming weeks. What are the rest of you looking forward to?

Yeah we just now attended one session on Microsoft Fabric, it was really exciting, and we also got to know the latest advanced technologies that we can look forward to. So it was really exciting to be part of that session. The line was a little too long to get into the session.

That is the challenging part, to get into the sessions, but for me the interesting part is AI and agents because, it is not a surprise, but to be outside of the SAP ecosystem is what I'm interested in too, and get to learn more of what's happening outside of SAP.

So have you tried to find any sessions without AI or agents in the name?

No, all agents. I think that was the qualifier, probably. OK, and for me it's actually not the first time I'm attending this conference. I was here in 2019 and it is really good to be back here. This time SAP has a big presence here, we are actually one of the sponsors, we have our place on the show floor, we have our sessions as Thomas mentioned, he's going to do one with Michael Ameling but as well we have some other SAP colleagues delivering sessions based on the experience from their work, working with UI for example, and with user experience in this exciting AI era. So all in all, "no coffee, no code" but unfortunately the line for coffee is the longest one here, so if you don't see me coding this week, that's just because I cannot stand in this line and get my coffee.

[DJ]
cds 10 is out! That is, the next major release of the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, also known as cds 10, was published last week.

This particular major release marks many milestones and is packed full of goodness.

Within the limitations of these news items, I can only give a brief overview, and you should definitely head over to the release notes which are very readable and full of details.

Some of the features and highlights are marked as Alpha or Beta ... so please pay attention to these status badges when working through the content.

Now, like other releases, the notes for this release are organised into different categories. There's the main major release upgrade category of course, a set of important highlights, notes on changes and improvements for both the Node.js and Java runtimes, and some updates for multitenancy services as well.

So, let's start with the very essence of this major release - the upgrade and migration context, and what tht implies.

Each of the packages that make up the entire framework have had major release version upgrades.
As the release notes say, don't mix old and new major versions. Instead, embrace change and try out the new cds upgrade tool which can help you.

The runtime support is also changing, with both Node.js and Java.

And before leaving this upgrade context, I must highlight the facilities that the CAP team have put together for this essential part of the software lifecycle - upgrades and migration. There's a new Migration Guide to help you get to cds 10 ... and a bleeding edge new 'cds upgrade' facility, in Alpha, to help with upgrades.

So next, let's have a look at some of the highlights, of which there are many in this release. Some of them are either in Alpha or Beta status, but they're there!

Let me enumerate them briefly. There's now support for aggregate data in hierarchies, useful for product catalogs, org charts, bills of materials and similar scenarios. It's available right now with the Java runtime, and support for Node.js is coming soon.

For VS Code users there's a new Project Explorer to help you visually navigate through your project and model's structures.

Now, while this has been hinted at before this advent of cds 10, there's a new protocol which I'm going to describe as "CAP native", and that's now available, and it's called HCQL - CQL conveyed over HTTP. Well suited for CAP-to-CAP integrations, and available just by adding an @hcql annotation, in the true CAP tradition of magic-made-simple.

There are early AI related goodies too, in the form of CAP-level (Calesi-style) agents, and also a CAP skills library, because, as we know, focusing on intent and best practice in development is essential.

There's a new Data Inspector, which comes in the form of a plugin and allows the controlled inspection of data in the entities exposed by your services.

Now, event queues in CAP have also been given lots of love, in the form of a new scheduling API, improved processing, and an overhaul of the documentation.

Now, last December we saw the first public Beta of the 'cds export' command which we can use to create API client packages from CDS service definitions. With this cds 10 release, that command is now generally available.

Now there are some more highlights in this cds 10 release, but my good friend and colleague Antonio is going to cover those in another news item in this news episode, so stay tuned!

Let me now turn to the Node.js runtime context where there are also plenty of items for us to celebrate.

First up, consolidated service APIs. What does that mean? Well, the generic CRUD (Create Read Update Delete) handler now returns the same shape of data for all write operations. That means app, db and HCQL-proxied remote services all look and behave the same way, which is great for uniform and minimised handler code variations.

Now, AI agents are great, but one thing they don't know much about is Fiori draft data. So working with active data has been simplified and consolidated - making it easier for everyone, us humans and our AI overlords, to work in a draft-first context.

A massive piece of work that the team has undertaken over the last months, culminating in what we have with cds 10, is a significant move towards a greater embrace of modern tools and native libraries. Very briefly - the testing framework Vitest is now used in favour of Jest, which will be abandoned longer term. ECMAScript Modules (that's ESM to you and me) is the official standardised system for packages and reuse, and CAP is now embracing that as the default module system. Third party dependencies are being dropped in favour of native or built-in alternatives. For example this calendar year already has seen a move to a Node-native SQLite driver (which replaces better-sqlite3) and to the Node-native Fetch API (which replaces Axios, and in development scenarios, also removes the need for the SAP Cloud SDK).

This work to embrace modern techniques, native libraries and the newest stable standards is fantastic. An important "side effect" is also something we can celebrate - a significantly reduced set of dependencies (and thus a smaller install footprint and code surface area).

Now last but certainly not least in the Node.js category is some news on the Telemetry plugin, which is now at version 2 after a major update which also brings support for OpenTelemetry SDK 2.0.

Next, let's look at the Java runtime category, which has now reached major version 5. Lots of new features, including, importantly, support for Spring Boot 4.

There are some new minimum versions of major dependencies that you need to take care of.

With CAP Java 5 there are some changes that are not backwards compatible (as in any major release), but the migration is made easier for us with a specific migration guide and OpenRewrite recipes for automated code adjustments.

Now, there are changes to CDS property defaults to align them with recommended settings for production use.

The API methods that were deprecated in the previous major release and marked as 'forRemoval' ... have now been removed.

CAP Java 5 now defaults to JDK 25, which is a long-term-support version. The SapMachine 25 JDK with the SAP Java buildpack is recommended.

With this new major CAP Java release, search is harmonised with both CAP Node.js and RAP, and by default uses the SAP HANA search syntax (for which there is now full support). Fuzzy search is also now enabled by default on SAP HANA, with improved support for UUIDs and large strings, and available in hierarchy queries.

Now, there's also much faster typed data access available. Instead of using a dynamic proxy to handle the underlying data map for accessor interfaces, it's now possible to have implementation classes generated. This resulting in up to 10 times the performance.

Previously, CAP Java used Apache's Olingo library for OData support. Following Olingo's retirement last year, there's now built-in support for OData, that's no mean feat! That of course also means fewer dependencies and it also brings improved performance.

There's also support for creating active versions of draft-enabled entities, similar to what we learned about earlier in the Node.js runtime context.

There are also plenty more features that come with CAP Java 5. Check them out in the release notes.

But to round out the release notes overview for this cds 10 version, there are a couple of points of note in the context of MTX services. First, there's now a stricter annotation validation for extensions. And second, migration support to go from @SAP/cds-mtx to @SAP/cds-mtxs has been removed.

That pretty much wraps up things for this June 2026 major release, congratulations to the entire CAP team for a huge set of new features and improvements. And don't forget to check out Antonio's item in this news episode for those other highlights.

[Rich]
Hey friends, Rich Heilman here. I hope everyone is staying cool, staying out of the heat. Good news for ABAP developers this week. ABAP Cleaner is now available as a VS Code extension, bringing its powerful code formatting capabilities out of Eclipse and into the modern editor of choice for many developers. So, what does it do? ABAP Cleaner applies over 100 rules to your code with a single keyboard shortcut. Automatically cleaning up formatting, modernising syntax, and even flagging unused variables. The result is clean and consistent code across your entire team without anyone having to argue about style. You can run it as a quick one-shot format, or drop into interactive mode to choose exactly which rules apply to your codebase. If your team already has a configuration setup in Eclipse, you can import those rule variants directly. No need to start from scratch. The setup does a Java runtime and a bit of environment variable configuration, but once it's running, you get the same functionality that ABAP developers have relied on in ADT in Eclipse - now in a much lighter, faster editor. If you're doing ABAP development in VS Code, this one's worth installing. Check it out today.

[Ajay]
Hello everyone. Code Connect is next week, from 13th to 16th July 2026. For all of you who cannot join us in person, there are live streams available for UI5con and reCAP, from the main stage. Please check out the agenda and streaming links available for UI5con and reCAP and join us online during the Code Connect event. Links to the live streams and agendas will be available in the description.

[Antonio]
¡Hola, SAP Developers. As you already know, the June 2026 CAP release is huge. DJ has already shared many of the highlights and I just want to mention a couple more. There is a new AI plugin, in Alpha, which simplifies integration with SAP AI Core. It will automatically resolve service bindings, manage resource groups, and AI model deployments. And it provides a standard AI Core service to interact with AI Core programatically. The new Data Privacy plugin, in Beta, for Node.js, which provides out of the box integration with the SAP Data Privacy Integration Service. Based on the personal data and ILM annotations in the data model the plugin will automatically expose two endpoints that can be consumed by the service. Now, related to the integration space, CAP Node.js now supports the Event Mesh capability in SAP Integration Suite, as an alternative to the standalone SAP Event Mesh, meaning that you can now easily use it as an event broken in your CAP applications. There is the new MCP Protocol Adapter, which allows exposing existing CAP services to AI agents and LLM powered as just another protocol. Just install the plugin and annotate services with @McP, et voila, your CAP service exposes MCP functionality. Extremely powerful and easy to implement. I already used it in a CAP service that I'm developing while I was getting familiar with the new feature of SAP Integration Suite. Yes, what we've all been waiting for - the new MCP Gateway is now available as part of Integration Suite. It is part of the new Integration Cell Runtime which is a cloud native runtime purposely built for designing, deploying and operating APIs and MCP servers. It is GA now and being rolled out in the Enhanced and Premium editions of SAP Integration Suite. Not just that, but it is also available in the BTP Trial environment. What are yoiu waiting for? Go check it out. Hasta luego!

[Ajay]
Did you know that cds add does generate the best practices of the test implementation of your CAP service? One can rapidly create a sample CAP project with cds init bookshop --add sample. Run cds add test in the terminal to add test suite files for all the defined CAP services in your existing project. It creates the latest boilerplate files and structures required for you to get started with your test implementation in a jiffy. For each services it generates a sample test for the GET request for an entity and a test for any action defined in the service. This also adds the test dependencies to your project. It uses the Node test runner for the tests. Install your dependencies and just run npm t to execute your tests. The generated test implementation does need to be tweaked for the valid test data. CAP also provides a command npx cds test to mute the verbose server output and lets you focus only on the failing tests. Use -u to unmute the server output when you do not need them. Enjoy your CAP tests!

entry.title: From SEGW to @OData.publish: Expose Your CDS View as OData in S/4HANA
entry.content:
Introduction:

This Blog2 is continuation of blog 1, refer below link:
Link to blog1 
 
In Blog1, we created a CDS view using tables Sales Order Header (VBAK) and Sales Order Items (VBAP). Now, in this Blog, we will make this data available for outside world. 
In S/4HANA, CDS views are not just used for reporting - they can also be exposed as OData service, which allows them to be consumed by external systems, SAP Fiori applications, API and integrations.
 
As an ABAP developer I have always used transaction code SEGW to manually create OData services. But now with CDS, this process is much simpler and faster.

 

Lets see how to expose CDS view as OData : Step by Step:

Step 1: Add OData Annotation

Open your CDS view and add anootation @odata.publish: true
Example below
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZSALEORDREP'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'Creating my 1st CDS view'
@OData.publish: true

define view ZCDS_ORDVIEW
    as select from vbak as Header
  association [1..*] to vbap as Item
    on Header.vbeln = Item.vbeln
{
  key Header.vbeln as SalesOrder,
      Header.erdat as CreationDate,
      Header.netwr as NetAmount,
      Header.waerk as Currency,

      case Header.auart
        when '00' then 'Standard Order'
        else 'Other Order Type'
      end as OrderTypeDescription,

      Item
}

Step 2: Activate the CDS View

Press ctrl + F3 or icon activate to activate the CDS view. Once activated, SAP will generate the OData service with name <cdsviewname>_CDS.

In our example OData service name generated is ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS .

Step 3: Activate the Service in SAP Gateway

Even though the service is generated, it must be activated in gateway system. Login to your gateway system and follow below steps:

  1. Go to transaction /n/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE
  2. Click Add Service
  3. Enter system alias as: your system name
  4. Search service: ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS .
  5. Once its found, select the service and click button 'Add selected Services'.
  6. Assign a package. In my case I used local package $TMP.

 

 
 

Step 4: Test the OData Service

To test the OData service, in gateway system run transaction code /n/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE then it will list down all the services name under technical service name. Select your service ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS and click button SAP Client Gateway.  This will open new screen SAP gateway client. Refer below screenshots:
 
 

$metadata shows full structure like Fields, types, associations

Example: Use the following URL to filter a specific sales order: /sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$filter=SalesOrder eq '00XXXXX178' Note: Ensure there are no spaces around the '=' in the $filter parameter.


 /sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW/$count

You can also use different OData query options like Top 5 records: ...?$top=5, Sort data: ...?$orderby=CreationDate desc etc. These options help in optimizing data retrieval and are widely used in Fiori applications.

To test using URL, use below format. For testing I have used Postman :

https://<host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$top=5

 
The OData service can also be tested outside of SAP using tools like: - Browser,  Postman or Curl Example: https://<host>/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZCDS_ORDVIEW_CDS/ZCDS_ORDVIEW?$top=5

When you add @OData.publish: true:

  • SAP automatically creates an OData service
  • Associations become navigation properties
  • CDS fields are exposed as OData properties
  • No manual service modeling is required

This is why CDS is preferred in modern development.

Conclusion
